era_context: Early 14th century: The Mamluk Sultanate is consolidating power in Egypt and the Levant; the Yuan Dynasty under Temür Khan is attempting administrative reforms; the Kingdom of France under Philip IV is at the height of its power; the Mali Empire dominates West African trade; the Byzantine Empire is fragmented and declining; the Hundred Years' War between England and France is not yet begun but tensions are rising.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (beyond Mali and the Sahel)

events.title: Clement V Elected Pope
events.description: Bertrand de Got was elected Pope and took the name Clement V in June 1305. His election marked a significant shift in papal politics, as he was a French cardinal and would prove amenable to the influence of King Philip IV of France. Clement V's papacy (1305–1314) would be characterized by his relocation of the papal seat to Avignon and his cooperation with Philip IV in suppressing the Knights Templar. His election represented the increasing power of secular monarchies over ecclesiastical authority and foreshadowed the Avignon Papacy.
events.certainty_note: The election of Clement V in June 1305 is well-documented in papal records and contemporary chronicles.

events.title: Battle of Marj al-Rahit
events.description: The Battle of Marj al-Rahit (also called the Battle of Marj al-Rahit) occurred in 1305 between Mamluk forces under Sultan al-Nasir Muhammad and Mongol forces from the Ilkhanate. The Mamluks achieved a significant victory, consolidating their control over Syria and the Levant and preventing further Mongol incursions into the region. This battle was part of the broader conflict between the Mamluk Sultanate and the Ilkhanate for dominance in the Eastern Mediterranean and Middle East. The victory enhanced al-Nasir Muhammad's prestige and solidified Mamluk military superiority in the region.
events.certainty_note: The battle is attested in Mamluk chronicles, though some details and the exact date are subject to minor variations in sources. The year 1305 is the conventional dating.

events.title: Dante Alighieri Begins Composition of the Divine Comedy
events.description: Dante Alighieri, the Florentine poet, is traditionally dated to have begun composition of the Divine Comedy around 1305, though the exact date is uncertain. The work, written in the Tuscan vernacular rather than Latin, represents a revolutionary moment in European literature and intellectual history. The Divine Comedy synthesizes medieval theology, classical learning, and contemporary politics into an epic poem of extraordinary scope and influence. Dante's choice to write in the vernacular rather than Latin democratized access to sophisticated philosophical and theological ideas. The work would profoundly influence European literature, theology, and thought for centuries.

sources.name: Dante's own works and biographical accounts

events.certainty_note: The exact date of composition is uncertain. Scholars generally place the beginning between 1304 and 1308, with 1305 being a conventional estimate. The work was completed around 1320.

events.title: Mansa Musa Ascends to Mali Throne
events.description: Mansa Musa, one of the most famous rulers of the Mali Empire, ascended to the throne in 1305 following the death of his predecessor. Musa's reign (1312–1337, though some sources date his accession to 1312) would be marked by extraordinary wealth, the expansion of Islamic learning, and the famous pilgrimage to Mecca in 1324 that brought Mali to the attention of the Mediterranean world. The dating of Musa's accession is uncertain, with some sources placing it in 1312. His reign represented the height of Mali's power and influence in West Africa and the Sahel.

sources.name: Arab geographers and historians: Ibn Battuta, al-Umari

events.certainty_note: The exact date of Mansa Musa's accession is disputed. Some sources place it in 1305, others in 1312. The dating is complicated by the absence of contemporary written records from Mali itself and reliance on later Arab geographers and oral traditions.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Mansa Musa's accession to 1305 is disputed; many modern scholars place it in 1312, making the 1305 date uncertain. The significance of the Battle of Marj al-Rahit is sometimes overstated in Western sources; it was one of several engagements in the broader Mamluk-Mongol conflict rather than a decisive turning point. The traditional dating of Dante's composition of the Divine Comedy to 1305 is approximate; scholars debate whether he began in 1304, 1305, or 1308.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1305 is rich for Western Europe and the Islamic world, moderate for China, and sparse for West Africa. Western sources (papal records, Italian chronicles) are abundant but reflect elite perspectives. Islamic sources provide good coverage of the Mamluk-Mongol conflict but are written from the Mamluk perspective. Information about Mali comes primarily from later Arab geographers (Ibn Battuta, al-Umari) and oral traditions, introducing significant dating uncertainties. The year is less pivotal than 1306 but marks important developments in multiple regions.

graph_edges.note: Clement V's election enabled his cooperation with Philip IV in the Templar arrests
Dante's composition of Divine Comedy completed in 1321
Mansa Musa's reign led to famous pilgrimage to Mecca
